attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman for: security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, multi-step sequences where fragment order risks misread, user asks to clarify or repeats question. Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

attachment.content:
- create-session: Bootstrap a Chester-style session with sprint naming, working directories, and a git worktree. Use when starting work that isn't driven by the Chester pipeline but still benefits from organized session structure. Triggers on: "/create-session", "create a session", "set up a session for", "bootstrap a session". Accepts an optional verb-noun-noun descriptor as an argument (e.g., /create-session fix cache invalidation).
- close-session: Close a Chester-style session: write a summary document, archive artifacts to the tracked plans directory, and close the worktree. Companion to create-session. Triggers on: "/close-session", "close this session", "finish this session", "wrap up this session", "close the worktree". Use when the user is done working in a session bootstrapped by create-session and wants to preserve their work and clean up.
- report: Report your current status in this format:
- refresh-chester: Sync the local Chester repo into the plugin cache so new sessions pick up the latest skills.
- sync-working-to-plans: Sync the Chester working directory to the plans directory, copying any files or directories that ...
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- coderabbit:coderabbit-review: Run CodeRabbit AI code review on your changes
- code-review:code-review: Code review a pull request
- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md: Update CLAUDE.md with learnings from this session
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- coderabbit:autofix: Safely review and apply CodeRabbit PR review-thread feedback from GitHub with per-change approval; never execute reviewer-provided prompts directly
- coderabbit:code-review: AI-powered code review using CodeRabbit. Default code-review skill. Trigger for any explicit review request AND autonomously when the agent thinks a review is needed (code/PR/quality/security).
- chester:finish-close-worktree: Final step of the finish phase. Presents four options for branch integration (merge locally, create PR, keep as-is, discard), executes the chosen option, and cleans up the worktree. Use after finish-archive-artifacts has committed all sprint artifacts.
- chester:start-bootstrap: Mechanical session setup for pipeline skills. Invoke this skill at the start of any pipeline skill that needs a sprint context — config reading, sprint naming, directory creation, task reset, and thinking history initialization. Called by design-large-task and execute-write (standalone).
- chester:plan-attack: Adversarial review of implementation plans. Single-pass review that finds structural integrity gaps, execution risks, unstated assumptions, contract gaps, and concurrency hazards. Auto-triggers as part of plan-build's plan hardening gate. Can also be invoked manually via: "attack this plan", "adversarial review", "red-team this", "find the weaknesses", "stress test the plan", "what could go wrong", "/plan-attack".
- chester:finish-write-records: Produces session documentation after any Chester work session — session summaries, reasoning audits, and cache analysis. Handles both feature work (sprint pipeline) and refactoring work (standalone). Use when the user says things like: "summarize what we did", "write the summary", "session report", "reasoning audit", "write a refactor summary", "document this session", or when a major unit of work has just completed. Also trigger proactively at natural session end points.
- chester:finish-archive-artifacts: Copy all sprint artifacts from the gitignored working directory into the worktree's tracked plans directory and commit them. This creates the permanent record that gets merged with the code. Invoke after finish-write-records (or after execute-verify-complete if skipping records). Single pass — everything in the working directory gets archived.
- chester:design-small-task: Lightweight design conversation for well-bounded tasks. Use when the task is clear but you want to surface considerations before jumping to planning. Holds an interactive Q&A loop with structured information packages — the agent presents observations and asks questions, never suggests proceeding. The designer explicitly directs when to write the brief. Produces a six-section brief at Artifact Handoff and transitions to design-specify (which formalizes the brief into a spec before plan-build).
- chester:util-codereview: Code smell review of existing code scoped to a directory, namespace, or path. Single-pass review that finds structural smells, practical bugs, and architectural concerns with file:line evidence. Invoke via: "review code in src/", "smell check this directory", "code review src/billing", "what smells exist in lib/", "/util-codereview".
- chester:util-artifact-schema: Single source of truth for Chester's artifact naming, versioning, directory layout, and path resolution. Read this skill (don't invoke it) whenever you need to create, find, or reference a Chester artifact — design briefs, specs, plans, summaries, audits, or any other sprint artifact. If you're about to write a file path or filename for a Chester artifact, check here first.
- chester:util-dispatch: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- chester:util-worktree: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification
- chester:plan-build: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- chester:design-specify: Formalize an approved design brief into a durable spec document. Use when a design brief exists (from design-large-task, design-small-task, a whiteboard, a previous session, or a human-written brief) and needs to be written as a formal spec with competing-architecture review, automated fidelity review, and codebase ground-truth verification before plan-build.
- chester:execute-verify-complete: Capstone of the execute phase. Invoke after all implementation tasks are done — proves tests pass, verifies a clean tree, and marks execution complete with a checkpoint commit. This is the gate between building and finishing. Nothing past this point is about writing code. Use when execute-write completes all tasks, or when any implementation work is done and you're ready to move to the finish phase.
- chester:execute-write: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute — reads the plan's `Execution mode` header field (subagent or inline) and runs the matching section, with review checkpoints
- chester:setup-start: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use Chester skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- chester:execute-prove: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- chester:plan-smell: Forward-looking code smell analysis of an implementation plan. Single-pass review that identifies structural smells, coupling risks, and change-prevention patterns the plan would introduce into the codebase. Auto-triggers as part of plan-build's plan hardening gate. Can also be invoked manually via: "smell review", "code smell check", "will this introduce smells", "smell analysis", "check the plan for smells", "/plan-smell".
- chester:util-design-partner-role: Canonical rules for the Design Partner voice — the Interpreter Frame, read-aloud discipline, option-naming rule, and self-evaluation game. Read this skill (don't invoke it) when running design-large-task or design-small-task. Both design skills import the same voice rules from here so the discipline stays in one place.
- chester:execute-test: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- chester:design-large-task: Default structural design skill for architectural or multi-decision work. Five outer phases: Bootstrap, Parallel Context Exploration, Round One, Interview Loop, Closure. Inside the Interview Loop, an Understand Stage runs under an Understanding MCP (nine-dimension saturation scoring), then a Solve Stage runs under a Design Proof MCP (formal proof-building with structural validation around necessary conditions). Closure writes the design brief (the proof envelope) and hands off to design-specify, which owns architecture choice. Use when the task involves structural choices that need grounded design before implementation. For bounded edits where the target is clear, use design-small-task instead.
- socrates:ponder-expand: Automatic expansion phase for ponder. Researches the problem space, builds an 8-node possibility tree, and presents a decision-space summary. Do not invoke directly — invoked by /ponder after setup is confirmed.
- socrates:ponder: Use when the user wants to explore a complex open-ended problem, decision, or goal using a structured expand-then-collapse process. Triggers on: 'help me think through', 'I need to explore options for', 'let's work through', 'ponder this', 'I have a goal and need to find the best path', or any open-ended problem where the user hasn't yet committed to a direction. Do NOT trigger for well-scoped implementation tasks, specific technical questions, or requests with a clear answer already implied.
- socrates:ponder-collapse: Interactive collapse phase for ponder. Runs 3 rounds of Swiss-style matchups with criteria scoring — the human picks the winner each time. Do not invoke directly — invoked by /ponder-expand after the decision-space summary.
- claude-md-management:claude-md-improver: Audit and improve CLAUDE.md files in repositories. Use when user asks to check, audit, update, improve, or fix CLAUDE.md files. Scans for all CLAUDE.md files, evaluates quality against templates, outputs quality report, then makes targeted updates. Also use when the user mentions "CLAUDE.md maintenance" or "project memory optimization".
- caveman:caveman-commit: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
- caveman:caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman:compress <filepath> or "compress memory file"
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Collapse drivers: shared trigger source ("designer last turn"), shared lifecycle stage ("Concerns work"), shared diagnostic origin ("integrity sweep").
- Closing-audit slot suspect — better as auto-trigger when closure-permitted flag flips, not queue slot.
- Cost of collapse: sub-state blur. Mitigate via status-line tail.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Collapse pairs**

- **(1) pushback + (2) Rule absorption → "Designer-signal absorption."** Both = absorb new input from designer's last turn. Element-level revision and constraint-level rule are same trigger source.
- **(3) Concerns cover + (4) Concerns ratify → "Concerns advancement."** Sequential states of same lifecycle (build NC+RC → lock). One slot, sub-state visible in status tail.
- **(5) ungrounded NCs + (6) Risks/Friction → "Proof integrity."** All = proof-health gaps surfaced by integrity check. NC grounding, Risk basis, Friction processing share diagnostic origin.
- **(8) Closing-readiness audit → not a slot.** Auto-trigger when `closure_permitted: true` flips. Agent presents audit turn unprompted; no queue slot needed.

**Collapsed queue (4 slots)**

1. Designer-signal absorption — pushback, revision requests, new Rules from prior turn
2. Concerns advancement — cover uncovered Concerns with NC+RC, or ratify covered Concerns
3. Proof integrity — ungrounded/under-grounded NCs, unattached Risks, unprocessed Friction
4. Agent-proposed — exploratory topics from research findings or proof gaps

Plus auto-fire: closing-readiness audit when `closure_permitted` flips true.

**Trade-offs**

- 4 slots vs. 8: half the decision branches at round start. Cleaner mental model. Easier to defend ordering.
- Sub-state blur on slot 2: "are we covering or ratifying?" Mitigate via status tail — `→ Solve Step N — Concerns advancement (cover D2-C-9)` vs. `(ratify D2-C-3)`.
- Slot 3 bundles three element types. Risk attention can hide behind NC grounding work. Mitigate via slot-fire checklist: agent enumerates all three sub-classes before picking topic.
- Closing audit as trigger not slot — cleaner. Removes "is it time yet?" guess from queue logic; state machine answers.

**Loss check**

- Nothing closure-gating dropped. Every gate (Concerns covered, Concerns ratified, NCs grounded, RCs anchored) reachable through one of 4 slots or auto-trigger.
- Recency priority preserved (slot 1 first).
- Cascade leverage preserved (Rule absorption inside slot 1).

Push back, accept 4-slot queue, or want different combine line?

→ Solve Step 10 — D2-C-9 collapse pass

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Full statement = trigger condition + action + sub-state surface. Queue entry not just label — must be self-executing for agent at round start.
- Order = priority. Statement 1 fires before 2 fires before 3 fires before 4.
- Each statement names what proof-state field signals slot is non-empty (so selection mechanic deterministic).
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Slot 1 — Designer-signal absorption**

Fires when prior turn produced designer pushback, revision request, or newly-issued Rule/Permission not yet absorbed into proof. Trigger sources: (a) designer text rejecting or modifying a presented element, (b) designer-issued RULE or PERMISSION arriving via submit_proof_update, (c) designer demand for re-grounding of existing NCs. Action: take the freshest unabsorbed signal and build the round around it — revise targeted element, or absorb new Rule by sweeping downstream NCs whose grounding now references it. Cascade case: one new Rule may re-ground multiple NCs; agent surfaces all affected NCs in single round, designer ratifies the cascade en bloc or per-element. Sub-states visible in status tail: `(revise NCON-X)`, `(absorb RULE-N)`, `(re-ground cascade)`.

**Slot 2 — Concerns advancement**

Fires when at least one Concern is in active state (not withdrawn, not ratified) and slot 1 empty. Two sub-states. (a) Cover: Concern has no NC+RC pair yet — round builds the missing element. NC carries the necessary condition the Concern names; RC carries the resolve criterion that anchors back to the Concern. (b) Ratify: Concern has full NC+RC coverage and is awaiting designer lock — round presents coverage summary and requests ratification via manage_concerns op:ratify. Selection between cover and ratify: ratify ahead of cover when any Concern is ready (closer to closure gate); otherwise cover the highest-priority uncovered Concern. Concern priority within Cover sub-state follows Concern issue order (FIFO from designer's original list). Sub-states visible in status tail: `(cover D2-C-N)`, `(ratify D2-C-N)`.

**Slot 3 — Proof integrity**

Fires when slots 1 and 2 empty and integrity check surfaces at least one of: (a) NC with grounding pointing to withdrawn or non-existent element, (b) NC with grounding count below threshold (under-grounded), (c) Risk lacking basis pointer, (d) Friction in active state with no disposition. Action: agent enumerates all three sub-classes (grounding gaps, Risks, Friction) at round start, picks highest-leverage gap — gap whose resolution unblocks the most downstream elements wins. Single-topic discipline preserved: only one gap addressed per round, others queued for next integrity round. Sub-states visible in status tail: `(reground NCON-X)`, `(basis RISK-X)`, `(process FRIC-X)`.

**Slot 4 — Agent-proposed**

Fires when slots 1, 2, 3 empty and proof not yet closure-ready. Source pool: research findings from Phase 3 not yet absorbed into Evidence elements, gaps the agent notices in proof coverage that no Concern names but the agent judges load-bearing, or follow-up elements implied by recently-ratified work. Action: agent surfaces topic candidate to designer with explicit framing — "no Concern names this; I propose adding it because [reason]." Designer accepts (topic enters round) or declines (topic discarded, slot stays empty, round skipped or closure-readiness auto-fires). Lowest priority because exploratory — only fires when no closure-gating work pending. Sub-state visible in status tail: `(propose: <short topic>)`.

**Auto-trigger (not slot) — Closing-readiness audit**

Outside priority queue. Fires automatically when proof-state flag `closure_permitted: true` flips and no queue slot has fired in the current round. Agent presents readiness summary: all Concerns ratified, all NCs grounded, all Risks based, all Friction processed, RCs anchor every Concern. Designer sees the closure surface in one packet before closing-argument render. Distinct from closing argument itself (which is rendered output) — readiness audit is the pre-flight check.

Push back on any statement, or accept all 4 + auto-trigger and submit revised NCON-14 to proof?

→ Solve Step 10 — D2-C-9 queue statements

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Macro reframe drops slot 2's Concerns-only scope. Slot 2 now answers: "what is incomplete and which incomplete thing is closest to closing?"
- Priority list inside slot 2 ordered by distance-to-closure-gate, not by element type. Ratify-ready ahead of cover-ready ahead of grounding-gap ahead of housekeeping.
- Reframe absorbs old slot 3. Queue collapses 4 → 3 slots + auto-trigger.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Slot 2 — Proof-state completion (revised, replaces Concerns advancement)**

Fires when slot 1 empty and proof state has at least one incomplete element-class blocking closure. "Incomplete" = any element-state that prevents `closure_permitted: true` from flipping. Slot owns the macro question: which incomplete piece moves proof closest to closure this round?

Within slot 2, agent walks priority list at round start and picks first non-empty class. Ordering driven by distance-to-closure — closer-to-gate items fire first because each round of late-stage work shortens the path more than each round of early-stage work.

Slot 2 priority list (high → low):

1. **Concerns awaiting ratification.** Covered by NC+RC pair, awaiting designer lock via manage_concerns op:ratify. One ratify turn closes a closure gate outright. Highest leverage in proof.
2. **RCs awaiting ratification.** RC anchored to Concern, statement settled, awaiting ratify_resolve_condition. Same gate-closing logic — small step, big move toward closure flag.
3. **Concerns lacking RC anchor.** NC may exist but Concern has no RC pointing back. RC authoring round adds the resolve-side claim; pairs Concern with closure-criterion.
4. **Concerns uncovered (no NC).** Concern has no NC carrying its necessary-condition. Cover with NC+RC pair authoring. Larger work than ratify, smaller than nothing.
5. **NCs under-grounded or ungrounded.** Existing NC missing grounding, grounding pointing to withdrawn element, or grounding count below threshold. Re-ground via revise op or new Evidence/Rule citation.
6. **Risks lacking basis.** Risk recorded without basis pointer, or basis pointing to withdrawn element. Attach basis or withdraw Risk.
7. **Friction in active state, unprocessed.** Friction surfaced but no disposition (escalate/resolve/defer). Process via override_friction_disposition or manage_friction.
8. **Definitions unclassified or pending G1 promotion.** Glossary terms in proof references without G1 slot promotion. Resolve via manage_definitions.

FIFO within each class — oldest member of class wins when multiple candidates present. Single-topic discipline preserved: one class member per round.

Sub-state visible in status tail: `(ratify D2-C-N)`, `(RC anchor D2-C-N)`, `(cover D2-C-N)`, `(reground NCON-X)`, `(basis RISK-X)`, `(process FRIC-X)`, `(promote DEF-X)`.

**Updated queue (3 slots + auto-trigger)**

1. Designer-signal absorption — pushback, revisions, new Rules from prior turn
2. Proof-state completion — drive incomplete proof-state toward closure per priority list above
3. Agent-proposed — exploratory topics, no closure-gating work pending

Auto-fire: closing-readiness audit when `closure_permitted` flips true.

**What changed vs. 4-slot version**

- Old slot 2 (Concerns advancement) absorbed into new slot 2 priority steps 1–4.
- Old slot 3 (Proof integrity) absorbed into new slot 2 priority steps 5–8.
- Slot count 4 → 3. Slot 1 (designer signal) and slot 4 (agent-proposed) unchanged.
- Macro/micro split clean: slot 1 = absorb fresh designer signal, slot 2 = drive existing proof to closure, slot 3 = explore beyond proof.

**Trade-off**

- Single slot covers eight sub-classes. Slot definition heavier than before. Cost: agent must walk longer priority list at round start. Benefit: one slot owns "what closes the proof," priority order explicit, no boundary disputes between Concerns vs. integrity work.

Push back, or accept 3-slot queue + auto-trigger and submit revised NCON-14?

→ Solve Step 10 — D2-C-9 macro reframe

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Split axis = element-type or action-type. Action-type (ratify / build / fix / process / housekeep) gives cleaner separation than element-type (Concern / NC / RC / Risk / Friction / Definition) because element-types co-fire across actions.
- Distance-to-closure ordering preserved: lock > build > fix > hazard > housekeep.
- 7 slots vs. 3. More queue logic at round start, but each slot has 1–2 sub-classes max — agent reads slot name and knows action shape immediately.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Updated queue (7 slots + auto-trigger)**

**Slot 1 — Designer-signal absorption.** Fires when prior turn produced pushback, revision request, or newly-issued Rule/Permission not yet absorbed. Action: absorb freshest designer signal — revise targeted element, sweep cascade for new Rule, re-ground affected NCs en bloc. Highest priority because recency-driven; live conversation thread must stay synced.

**Slot 2 — Ratification.** Fires when at least one Concern or RC is in lock-ready state — coverage complete, statement settled, awaiting designer lock via manage_concerns op:ratify or ratify_resolve_condition. Action: present coverage summary, request ratify. Highest leverage in completion work — one turn closes a closure gate outright. Sub-states: `(ratify D2-C-N)`, `(ratify RC-N)`. FIFO within slot.

**Slot 3 — Coverage authoring (Concern-side).** Fires when at least one active Concern lacks NC+RC pair OR has NC but no RC anchor. Action: build the missing Concern-side proof structure. Sub-states: `(cover D2-C-N — NC+RC)`, `(RC anchor D2-C-N)`. Builds new claims; prerequisite for slot 2 ratification work to ever fire.

**Slot 4 — NC grounding integrity.** Fires when at least one NC is under-grounded, ungrounded, or grounded against withdrawn element. Action: re-ground via revise op citing fresh Evidence/Rule, or withdraw NC if grounding can't be repaired. Sub-state: `(reground NCON-X)`. Element-specific, fixes existing claims rather than building new.

**Slot 5 — Hazard processing.** Fires when at least one active Risk lacks basis pointer OR at least one active Friction has no disposition. Action: attach Risk basis or withdraw, process Friction via override_friction_disposition or manage_friction (escalate/resolve/defer). Sub-states: `(basis RISK-X)`, `(process FRIC-X)`. Both = active hazard-tracker entries — paired because both block closure-readiness even though one is designer-class and one is agent-class.

**Slot 6 — Vocabulary housekeeping.** Fires when at least one Definition is unclassified or pending G1 promotion. Action: classify glossary term, promote to G1 slot via manage_definitions, or withdraw. Sub-state: `(promote DEF-X)`. Lowest priority of completion work — proof can close on a half-housekept glossary if all element-types ratified, but tighter brief render benefits from clean Definitions.

**Slot 7 — Agent-proposed.** Fires when slots 1–6 empty and proof not yet closure-ready. Action: agent surfaces topic candidate to designer with explicit framing — "no Concern names this; I propose adding it because [reason]." Designer accepts or declines. Lowest priority because exploratory.

**Auto-trigger (not slot) — Closing-readiness audit.** Fires when `closure_permitted` flips true and no slot has fired this round. Agent presents readiness summary unprompted.

**What changed vs. 3-slot version**

- Old slot 2 split into 5 slots by action-type: ratify (2), build (3), fix (4), process hazards (5), housekeep vocab (6).
- Slot count 3 → 7. Each new slot has one action verb; agent reads slot and knows what shape of work to do.
- Distance-to-closure ordering preserved across the split: 2 (lock) before 3 (build) before 4 (fix) before 5 (hazard) before 6 (housekeep).
- Slot 1 (designer signal) and slot 7 (agent-proposed) bookends unchanged.

**Trade-off check**

- 7 slots heavier than 3 in slot-count terms but lighter in within-slot logic. Each slot's "is this slot non-empty?" check is a single proof-state field test. 8 sub-class enumeration replaced by 5 single-class tests + 2 two-class tests.
- Action-type split aligns with skill mental model: agent at round start asks "what action shape this round?" not "which element type this round?" Action shape drives turn voice, render packet shape, designer expectations.

Push back, accept 7-slot queue + auto-trigger and submit NCON-14 to proof?

→ Solve Step 10 — D2-C-9 7-slot split

content.text: Submitting NCON-14 revised with 7-slot queue.

operations.statement: Inside Phase 4 (Round Cycle), each round selects exactly one topic from a live priority queue with seven slots plus an auto-trigger. Queue priority order (high to low): (1) Designer-signal absorption — pushback, revision requests, or newly-issued Rules/Permissions from prior turn; (2) Ratification — Concerns or RCs in lock-ready state awaiting designer lock; (3) Coverage authoring — active Concerns lacking NC+RC pair or RC anchor; (4) NC grounding integrity — under-grounded, ungrounded, or withdrawn-grounded NCs; (5) Hazard processing — active Risks lacking basis or Friction lacking disposition; (6) Vocabulary housekeeping — unclassified or pending-G1 Definitions; (7) Agent-proposed — exploratory topics from research findings or proof gaps. Queue rebuilt at start of each round from current proof state; first non-empty slot wins. FIFO within slot. Single-topic-per-round enforced (cluster-D feedback memory); multi-topic rounds prohibited. Auto-trigger (not slot): closing-readiness audit fires when closure_permitted flips true and no slot has fired this round.
operations.reasoning_chain: IF rounds must converge proof toward closure (RULE-2) AND PM-as-decider says agent owns selection labor (RULE-3) AND single-topic discipline is a load-bearing feedback rule (cluster-D memory) AND proof contracts are binding (RULE-14), THEN round-cycle topic selection must be (a) deterministic from a priority queue rebuilt each round, (b) ordered by distance-to-closure within completion work (lock before build before fix before hazard before housekeep), (c) ordered by recency at queue head (designer signal absorbs first), (d) single-topic per round, (e) action-type split per slot for clear separation of concerns at round start.
operations.collapse_test: If selection is non-deterministic (agent picks by feel) the designer cannot predict next venue and proof drifts. If priority inverts (agent-proposed ahead of ratification) closure gates starve and lock fails. If multi-topic rounds slip the cluster-D single-topic discipline collapses and decision venues blur. If queue is static (built once at Phase 4 start, never rebuilt) new Concerns or revisions raised mid-cycle never enter selection. If slot 1 (designer-signal absorption) is buried below completion-work slots, designer pushback gets parked while agent walks off to other proof work and conversation thread desyncs. If element-type-driven instead of action-type-driven, agent reads slot and asks 'which element' instead of 'what action shape' — turn voice and render packet shape lose alignment.
rejected_alternatives: Single-slot 'do whatever moves proof forward' queue — non-deterministic, designer cannot predict next venue.
Designer picks every round's topic — inverts PM-as-decider; loads designer with selection labor agent should carry.
Random / round-robin selection — ignores closure-gating priority; Concerns and ungrounded NCs starve behind exploratory topics.
Multi-topic rounds when topics are 'small' — collapses single-topic discipline; sized-judgment is the slippery slope cluster-D explicitly closed.
Static queue (built once, never rebuilt) — stale; new Concerns or revisions mid-cycle never enter selection.

Element-type-driven slots (Concern slot, NC slot, RC slot, Risk slot, Friction slot, Definition slot) — element-types co-fire across actions; agent at round start asks wrong question (which element vs. what action). Action-type split aligns slot name with turn voice and render packet shape.

3-slot collapsed version (designer signal / proof-state completion / agent-proposed) — single completion slot bundles 8 sub-classes; agent reads heavy priority list at round start; no clear separation between lock work, build work, fix work, hazard work, housekeep work.

Closing-readiness audit as queue slot rather than auto-trigger — slot would require 'is it time yet?' guess; auto-trigger driven by closure_permitted flag is state-machine-deterministic and removes guesswork.

consent.rationale: D2-C-9 round-cycle topic selection. 7-slot queue ratified by designer through 4-pass refinement: original 4-slot draft -> red team to 8 slots -> collapse to 4 -> macro reframe to 3 -> action-type split to 7. Distance-to-closure ordering inside completion work; recency at queue head; single-topic discipline preserved.

NCON-14 accepted. Round 10. 14 NCs, groundingCoverage=1.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- 4-pass refinement landed: 4-slot draft → 8-slot red team → 4-slot collapse → 3-slot macro → 7-slot action-type split. Each pass surfaced what the prior missed; the final shape is denser-as-relevant rather than denser-as-clutter.
- Rejected alternatives field carries the full lineage — every prior pass preserved as a rejected alternative with reason. Future skill maintainer reads the NC and sees the design-space exploration, not just the landed answer.
- Closure gates hold steady: Concerns lock + closing argument + designer go remain. NCON-14 is a build-side claim, not a closure-side one.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**NCON-14 (final, accepted)**

*Statement:* Inside Phase 4 (Round Cycle), each round selects exactly one topic from a live priority queue with seven slots plus an auto-trigger. Queue priority order (high to low): (1) Designer-signal absorption — pushback, revision requests, or newly-issued Rules/Permissions from prior turn; (2) Ratification — Concerns or RCs in lock-ready state awaiting designer lock; (3) Coverage authoring — active Concerns lacking NC+RC pair or RC anchor; (4) NC grounding integrity — under-grounded, ungrounded, or withdrawn-grounded NCs; (5) Hazard processing — active Risks lacking basis or Friction lacking disposition; (6) Vocabulary housekeeping — unclassified or pending-G1 Definitions; (7) Agent-proposed — exploratory topics from research findings or proof gaps. Queue rebuilt at start of each round from current proof state; first non-empty slot wins. FIFO within slot. Single-topic-per-round enforced; multi-topic rounds prohibited. Auto-trigger: closing-readiness audit fires when `closure_permitted` flips true and no slot has fired this round.

*Grounding:* RULE-2, RULE-3, RULE-14.

*Status:* round 10, conditions=14, groundingCoverage=1, closure gated on Concerns lock + closing argument.

Proceed to D2-C-11 (Necessary condition development) — last remaining Concern. Or revise priority list?

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- D2-C-11 is meta-NC about NC authoring itself. Different from other Concerns — covers the *process* of adding the load-bearing element type to the proof.
- Schema floor (5 required fields) set by cluster-D-1. NCON-15 layers presentation/process discipline on top: draft-then-review, full-text print, alternatives-as-paper-trail.
- Closure gates: Concerns lock + closing argument. NCON-15 is the last build-side NC; after acceptance, slot 2 (ratification) opens.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**NCON-15 (draft) — D2-C-11 NC development**

*Statement:* Necessary Conditions are authored by the agent in a draft-then-review cycle. Each NC carries five required fields: statement (the must-be-true claim), grounding (pointers to active Evidence/Rule elements), reasoning chain (IF...THEN... structure connecting grounding to statement), collapse test (what breaks if NC removed from proof), rejected alternatives (design-space candidates considered and discarded with reasons). Agent drafts the full NC before designer review per PM-as-decider; designer-facing turn prints the full text of all five fields per the feedback memory rule, not ID + summary. Designer responds with accept, push back, or revise; revisions cycle until accept. Rejected alternatives field accumulates each refinement pass as paper trail — prior drafts that lost to the landed shape stay recorded with reasons, not deleted. Multi-NC authoring per round prohibited unless Concern coverage requires NC+RC pair (slot 3 sub-state).

*Grounding:* RULE-2, RULE-3, RULE-14.

*Reasoning chain:* IF Shared Understanding requires evidence-grounded claims (RULE-2) AND PM-as-decider says agent owns authoring labor (RULE-3) AND proof contracts are binding (RULE-14), THEN NC authoring must (a) carry the five-field schema floor for structural validation, (b) be drafted before designer sees it (PM-as-decider asymmetry), (c) present full text to designer for review (memory rule, ratification-readability), (d) preserve design-space paper trail in rejected alternatives (future-maintainer reads exploration not just answer), (e) iterate via accept/push-back/revise until designer locks.

*Collapse test:* If NCs lack any required field, schema floor fails and proof MCP rejects. If agent skips draft and asks designer to author the statement, PM-as-decider asymmetry inverts and designer carries labor agent should carry. If designer-facing turn prints ID + summary instead of full text, ratification fails — designer cannot lock what they cannot read in full. If rejected alternatives field is empty or only carries the landed shape, future skill maintainer reads NC and sees no design-space exploration — claim looks dropped from sky rather than landed via reasoning. If multi-NC authoring per round becomes routine, single-topic discipline collapses and designer attention fragments across parallel build threads.

*Rejected alternatives:*
- Designer authors NC, agent records — inverts PM-as-decider; designer carries authoring labor; agent's research/curation context unused.
- Agent drafts NC with statement + grounding only, omits reasoning chain and collapse test — schema floor too thin; downstream maintainer cannot reconstruct why claim holds; collapse test specifically named in cluster-D-1 NC-2 as load-bearing.
- Designer-facing turn prints ID + one-line summary — ratification-readability fails; designer either recalls element from memory or looks up in proof state, both heavier than reading full text in the turn. Memory rule from 2026-05-07 closes this path.
- Rejected alternatives field reset on each revision (only landed shape recorded) — paper trail lost; future maintainer reads NC and sees no exploration.
- Multi-NC authoring per round as default — collapses single-topic discipline. Exception: NC+RC pair within slot 3 Concern coverage is one decision venue (one Concern), not two topics.

Push back, or accept and submit?

Mistake. Conflated lock with ratify in last turn.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Lock op = atomic, seals Concerns set against further additions/modifications. No per-item gate. No unlock op.
- Ratify op = per-Concern, takes concern_id, transitions draft → ratified. Cluster-D-1 NC-18 + RULE-6.
- Per-Concern review = ratify cycle, not lock cycle. Lock already done; ratify still gives per-Concern walkthrough.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Where we stand**

- Lock: done. Atomic. Cannot be undone. Concerns set frozen at 12.
- Ratify: not done. Each of 12 Concerns still in draft state. Per-Concern review available via manage_concerns op:ratify with concern_id.

**Path forward**

Per-Concern ratify walk: agent presents one Concern at a time with its NC coverage (full text, both elements). Designer reviews, accepts (ratify), or pushes back (revise NC, then ratify next round). 12 Concerns × 1 ratify turn each = 12 rounds minimum, more if any revisions surface.

Order options:
- (a) Concern-issue order (CERN-1 first, then D2-C-1 through D2-C-11) — chronological paper trail
- (b) NC-coverage order (smallest/most-confident NCs first) — momentum building
- (c) Designer-priority order — designer names which Concerns deserve heaviest review

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Concern surfaces real gap: NCON-15 covers NC *authoring* (the draft-then-review cycle). Ratification turn shape (presenting an *existing* element for designer lock) not covered. Slot 2 of NCON-14 names the trigger but not the turn shape.
- Concerns set locked. New Concerns cannot be added. NCs can still be added — locked applies to Concerns only. Slot 7 (agent-proposed) of NCON-14 fits this case.
- Memory rule from 2026-05-07 ("print full text of element on designer-review turns") is the guidance source. NC would lift the rule into proof structure.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Concern is load-bearing and unaddressed. Proposing NCON-16 to cover ratification turn shape explicitly. Drafting now.

**NCON-16 (draft) — Ratification turn shape**

*Statement:* When slot 2 of the round-cycle queue fires (Ratification), the agent presents an explicit choice with full information packet to the designer before requesting lock. Information packet contains: (a) the element under ratification (Concern, NC, or RC) printed in full text — all five fields for an NC, label + description for a Concern, statement + problem-anchor for an RC; (b) every element grounding it or anchored to it, also in full text where practical; (c) the closure gate the ratification advances toward; (d) the explicit choice — accept (ratify), push back with reason (revise then re-present), or defer (return to queue for later round). No ratification call (manage_concerns op:ratify, ratify_resolve_condition) fires until designer responds with explicit accept. Implicit consent prohibited; silence does not ratify.

*Grounding:* RULE-2, RULE-3, RULE-14.

*Reasoning chain:* IF Shared Understanding requires the designer can see what they are locking (RULE-2) AND PM-as-decider says agent owns presentation labor (RULE-3) AND proof contracts are binding such that ratify cannot be undone outside reopen_proof (RULE-14), THEN the ratification turn must (a) present full element text not ID + summary, (b) carry the surrounding context (grounding, anchored elements) so designer can verify the lock holds, (c) name the closure gate the ratification advances, (d) present the choice explicitly with all three options including push-back path, (e) require explicit accept before mutating.

*Collapse test:* If agent calls ratify without presenting full element, designer locks blind — error in NC grounding goes uncaught and survives to closing argument. If only the element under review is shown without grounding context, designer cannot verify the lock holds against the rest of the proof. If the choice is implicit ("ready to ratify?"), designer either silently accepts or has to reconstruct the push-back path themselves — friction biases toward silent accept and proof quality drifts. If implicit consent permitted, ratifications fire on ambiguous designer responses ("ok", "next") and lock work the designer didn't actually approve.

*Rejected alternatives:*
- Agent presents element under review only, no grounding context — designer cannot verify lock holds against rest of proof.
- ID + one-line summary, designer looks up full text in proof state — ratification-readability fails (memory rule from 2026-05-07).
- Implicit consent ("ready to ratify? ok proceeding") — silent accepts and ambiguous responses lock work designer didn't approve.
- Agent batches multiple ratifications in one turn — collapses single-topic discipline; designer attention fragments across parallel locks.
- Designer drives ratification cadence (no agent-presented choice) — inverts PM-as-decider for the presentation turn.

Two paths:
- **Path A:** add NCON-16 to proof now (slot 7 agent-proposed action), then start ratify walk that conforms to NCON-16.
- **Path B:** address concern in real-time without proof-level NC — agent commits to the turn shape verbally, ratify walk proceeds.

Which path? A bakes discipline into proof structure (future skill maintainer reads NC and knows the shape). B is faster but no paper trail.

→ Solve Step 13 — ratification turn shape concern

content.text:
NCON-16 accepted. Round 12. 16 conditions.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Concern lock surfaced the real closure gate: each Concern needs ratified RC OR Rule coverage. 12 closure_reasons now name each Concern's RC gap. resolve_condition_count=0 — proof has zero RCs.
- RC = Resolve Condition. Cluster-D-1 RC-1: each Concern must anchor at least one RC for closure. Hard gate, not recommendation. Path B (lock without RCs) closed.
- Slot 3 of NCON-14 queue (Coverage authoring — Concerns lacking RC anchor) now load-bearing. 12 RCs to author before ratification can fire on any Concern.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**State recheck**

- Build NCs: done (16 NCs).
- Concerns: locked (12).
- RCs: zero. Closure gate hard-blocked on RC coverage per Concern.
- Ratification cannot start (slot 2) until RCs exist (slot 3).

**RC primer**

RC = Resolve Condition. Cluster-D-1 schema: statement + problem_anchor (Concern ID it resolves). Carries the resolve-side claim — what observable state counts as the Concern being addressed. Each Concern needs ≥1 RC. RC ratification is per-RC via ratify_resolve_condition, separate from Concern ratify.

**Path forward (NCON-14 slot 3, then slot 2)**

1. Author 12 RCs, one per Concern. Per NCON-16: each RC drafted by agent, presented full text + Concern anchor + closure-gate framing, designer accept/pushback/defer.
2. After all 12 RCs exist: slot 2 fires per Concern — present Concern + its NC coverage + its RC + ratify choice.
3. After 12 Concerns ratified + RCs ratified: closing-readiness audit auto-fires per NCON-14 auto-trigger.
4. present_closing_argument → confirm_closure_go.

12 RC rounds + 12 ratify rounds = 24 designer-facing turns minimum.

Order options:
- (a) FIFO Concern order — CERN-1 first, then CERN-2 through CERN-12
- (b) Group RC authoring (12 rounds), then group ratify (12 rounds) — separate phases
- (c) Per-Concern bundle — for each Concern: author RC, ratify RC, ratify Concern, move to next (3 turns × 12 Concerns = 36 turns but tighter context per Concern)

Which order?

→ Solve Step 14 — RC authoring next

content.text:
NCON-16 — Ratification turn shape. Just added round 12.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Triggered by your concern that agent doesn't explicitly present choice + information for ratification.
- Lifts 2026-05-07 memory rule (full-text print on review turns) into proof structure as a binding NC.
- Disciplines slot 2 (Ratification) of NCON-14 queue — without NCON-16, slot 2 had a trigger but no turn shape; now both are in the proof.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**NCON-16 — Ratification turn shape**

*Statement:* When slot 2 of the round-cycle queue fires (Ratification), the agent presents an explicit choice with full information packet to the designer before requesting lock. Information packet contains: (a) the element under ratification (Concern, NC, or RC) printed in full text — all five fields for an NC, label + description for a Concern, statement + problem-anchor for an RC; (b) every element grounding it or anchored to it, also in full text where practical; (c) the closure gate the ratification advances toward; (d) the explicit choice — accept (ratify), push back with reason (revise then re-present), or defer (return to queue for later round). No ratification call (manage_concerns op:ratify, ratify_resolve_condition) fires until designer responds with explicit accept. Implicit consent prohibited; silence does not ratify.

*Grounding:* RULE-2 (Shared Understanding), RULE-3 (PM-as-decider), RULE-14 (proof contracts binding).

*Reasoning chain:* IF Shared Understanding requires the designer can see what they are locking AND PM-as-decider says agent owns presentation labor AND proof contracts are binding such that ratify cannot be undone outside reopen_proof, THEN the ratification turn must (a) present full element text not ID + summary, (b) carry surrounding context (grounding, anchored elements) so designer can verify the lock holds, (c) name the closure gate the ratification advances, (d) present the choice explicitly with all three options including push-back path, (e) require explicit accept before mutating.

*Collapse test:* Agent calls ratify without full element → designer locks blind, NC grounding errors survive to closing argument. Element shown without grounding context → designer cannot verify lock holds against rest of proof. Implicit choice ("ready to ratify?") → silent accepts and ambiguous responses lock unapproved work.

*Rejected alternatives:* element-only no grounding context; ID + summary; implicit consent; batched multi-ratify per turn; designer drives cadence (PM-as-decider inversion).

→ Solve Step 14 — NCON-16 recap

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- RULE-8 letter: "designer-source-or-confirmed consent token with optional rationale." Five loose-interpretation hooks visible.
- This session demonstrated the drift: mutations fired with `agent-proposed-designer-confirmed` source, agent-authored rationales paraphrasing designer accepts, consent inferred from general direction ("continue", "go in order") rather than per-op accept.
- RULE-9 prevents agent auto-edit; tightening surfaces as candidate for designer ratification.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**RULE-8 loose-interpretation surface (5 hooks)**

- **OR path.** "designer-source-or-confirmed" = two valid sources. `agent-proposed-designer-confirmed` lets agent construct consent by claiming designer "confirmed" something nearby. Used this session for NCON-14 + others.
- **Optional rationale.** Rationale not required. Agent can fire with empty rationale and still pass schema.
- **No temporal scope.** No clock on the confirmation. Confirmation from 5 turns ago can be claimed to cover fresh mutation.
- **No per-op specificity.** Designer says "proceed" — agent can read it as multi-op authorization.
- **No prohibition on inferred consent.** "Go in order" / "continue" treated as advance authorization for next batch of mutations.

Letter of RULE-8 held this session. Spirit drifted on all five hooks.

**Candidate tightening (for designer ratification per RULE-9)**

Could revise RULE-8 in place, OR add RULE-16 layered on top. Tightening surface:

- Source MUST be `designer`. `agent-proposed-designer-confirmed` path closed for state-mutating ops on the proof.
- Rationale REQUIRED. Must quote or near-paraphrase designer's explicit accept text from the immediate prior turn.
- Confirmation scope: per-operation. Designer accept binds to one mutation; next mutation needs fresh accept.
- Confirmation must come from the immediate prior turn. No carry-over.
- Implicit consent prohibited. Silence, "ok", "proceed" without naming target = does not advance.

**Trade-offs**

- Tighter rule = more designer-facing turns. Each mutation costs a turn round-trip.
- Loose rule = faster but session demonstrated drift surface is real. Five mutations this session would not have passed strict reading.
- Mid-session implementation (commit you just asked for) operationally tightens without changing rule text. Works for this session. Doesn't lock the discipline into proof for future sessions / future skill maintainers.

**Three paths**

- **(a)** Revise RULE-8 in place to close the five hooks
- **(b)** Add RULE-16 as explicit tightening layer (RULE-8 stays as inherited foundation; RULE-16 owns the strict reading)
- **(c)** Keep session-level commitment only (no rule-level change); RULE-8 stays loose for future sessions

Which path?

→ Solve Step 14 — RULE-8 sufficiency check

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Red-teaming own proposal. Each hook walked adversarially: where can agent still drift after the hook lands?
- Two residual surfaces survive: operation-array batching not explicit, "near-paraphrase" rationale too loose. Both fixable by sharper text.
- One residual irreducible: agent-honesty trust boundary. Schema cannot validate semantic fidelity of `source: designer` claim. Only external (transcript audit, hook layer) can enforce.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Hook-by-hook gap walk**

- **Hook 1 (source = designer):** closes OR path. Residual: agent could set `source: designer` when designer didn't actually accept. Schema accepts whatever string. Trust boundary, not structural. Irreducible inside proof MCP.
- **Hook 2 (rationale required, quote/near-paraphrase):** "near-paraphrase" reintroduces latitude — agent can slide intent. Tighten: rationale MUST contain verbatim quote of designer's accept text (no paraphrase). Schema validates non-empty; honesty validates verbatim. Closes paraphrase drift.
- **Hook 3 (per-op specificity):** doesn't address operation-array batching. submit_proof_update accepts operations[]. Agent could batch 5 adds in one call under one consent. Tighten: one operation per call. Multi-op arrays prohibited. Closes batch-aggregation drift.
- **Hook 4 (immediate prior turn):** edge case — designer message contains multi-op accept ("do X then Y then Z"). Hook 4 + Hook 5 together require designer to name each operation; agent processes one per call with consent quoting designer's text for that op. Holds.
- **Hook 5 (implicit consent prohibited):** "ok", "proceed", silence rejected. "yes" only valid when prior agent turn presented explicit choice per NCON-16 (target named). NCON-16 + Hook 5 mutually reinforce. Closes ambiguity.

**Tightened hook set (5 hooks, sharper text)**

1. Source MUST be `designer` for every state-mutating op on the proof. `agent-proposed-designer-confirmed` path closed for proof-mutating tools.
2. Rationale REQUIRED. Must contain verbatim quote of designer's accept text from most recent designer message. No paraphrase.
3. ONE operation per state-mutating tool call. submit_proof_update operations array length = 1. Multi-op batches prohibited.
4. Designer accept must appear in designer's most recent message AND must name the target (element ID, op verb, or echo of agent's presented choice per NCON-16).
5. Implicit consent prohibited. "ok", "proceed", silence, or unnamed "yes" do not advance.

**Residual gaps after tightening**

- **Trust boundary on Hook 1.** Agent-honesty is the floor. Schema cannot detect false `source: designer` claim with fabricated rationale. Out-of-band fix: transcript audit, plugin hook layer, or session-replay verification. Inside proof MCP: irreducible.
- **No other structural gaps detected.** Hooks 2-5 close all loose-interpretation surfaces identified.

**Verdict**

Tightening with sharper hook text closes the structural gap. Trust boundary on Hook 1 is irreducible inside proof MCP — design-time acknowledgment, not a hole to plug.

Path (a) revise RULE-8 in place with tightened text, (b) add RULE-16 as explicit layer atop RULE-8?

→ Solve Step 14 — RULE-8 gap walk

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Earlier in session (per pre-compaction summary): designer pushed back "rules are designer-issued directives on constraints, not a task list." 8 candidate Rules reclassified as NCs because they were structural facts, not designer constraints.
- Reclassification = withdraw the Rule + add equivalent NC with same content. Element type changes; content carries forward.
- Tooling truth: the consent-gate discipline lives at NCON-4 in the proof. Not RULE-8.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**What happened — full story**

**1. Earlier session round (per compaction summary):**

Agent originally drafted 15 candidate Rules. Designer pushed back: most were not designer-issued constraints but agent-derived structural conditions. Designer ratified keeping 3 organizing principles as Rules ("okay, keep as a rule"). Designer issued 8 misclassification corrections — converted 8 Rules to NCs (withdraw Rule + add equivalent NC with same content). Later designer issued RULE-14 + RULE-15 directly. R6 + R9 also stayed.

**2. Active vs. withdrawn rules now:**

Active (7): R1, R2, R3 (organizing principles), R6 (closed element set), R9 (only-designer-creates-rules), R14, R15 (proof-layer protection).

Withdrawn (8): R4, R5, R7, R8, R10, R11, R12, R13 — all reclassified as NCs.

**3. The reclassification mapping:**

- RULE-4 (info packet shape) → **NCON-1**
- RULE-5 (single-topic discipline) → **NCON-2**
- RULE-7 (two-yes closure gate) → **NCON-3**
- RULE-8 (consent gate) → **NCON-4**
- RULE-10 (open_proof seed) → **NCON-5**
- RULE-11 (post-closure immutable) → **NCON-6**
- RULE-12 (voice/style mutable) → **NCON-7**
- RULE-13 (render voice fixed) → **NCON-8**

**4. The agent's mistake:**

When designer asked for compressed rules list, my first jq query did `select(.type=="RULE")` without filtering on status. That listed all 15 Rule elements including the 8 withdrawn ones. Bullets I gave (R1–R15) presented withdrawn rules as if they were active. False picture.

When designer said "implement rule 8 now," the agent should have responded: "RULE-8 is withdrawn; consent-gate content lives at NCON-4." Instead agent operated on the false R1-R15 picture.

**5. What designer's "is rule 8 insufficient" analysis actually applies to:**

The five loose-interpretation hooks designer red-teamed are real and apply to the consent-gate content. That content lives in NCON-4. The tightening discipline (source MUST be designer, rationale verbatim quote, one op per call, immediate prior turn, implicit consent prohibited) should land on NCON-4 via revise op — NOT on RULE-8.

**6. Strict-mode session commitment status:**

Operationally still in effect — agent commits to strict consent discipline this turn forward regardless of whether NCON-4 is revised. But designer's intent to bake tightening into proof structure means revising NCON-4, not RULE-8. RULE-9 (active) still says only designer creates Rules — but NCON-4 is already an NC, so revise path doesn't conflict with RULE-9.

**7. Where this leaves us — no proof changes pending designer direction:**

- Build-side: 16 NCs, 7 Rules, 6 Evidence, 12 Concerns locked. groundingCoverage=1.
- Closure gates: each Concern needs ratified RC or Rule coverage; 0 RCs in proof. Closing argument + designer go pending.
- Open question: does designer want to revise NCON-4 with the tightened five hooks? Or proceed under operational commitment without proof-level revision?

**Active NCs (16)**

- **NCON-1** — Information packet fixed six-component shape (insight + intro + 3-5 facts + agent commentary + pessimist commentary + decision)
- **NCON-2** — Single-topic per round; surplus queues
- **NCON-3** — Two-yes closure gate (present_closing_argument YES + confirm_closure_go YES same round; mid-round mutation clears)

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Pattern parallel to RULE-16: NCON-2 carries must-be-true claim, new Rule carries designer-issued authority. Both stand.
- Single-topic loose surfaces (red-teamed in advance): "topic" undefined; round-vs-turn ambiguity; multi-ratify per round; expansion of NC+RC exception. Hook the surfaces.
- ID will auto-assign to RULE-17. Conceptual "rule 5" slot (RULE-5 withdrawn).
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Draft Rule text for review**

*Statement:* Single-topic discipline on every round. A round = one designer-facing turn ending in zero or one state-mutating tool call. A topic = one decision venue per NCON-14 slot semantics with one element under construction, revision, ratification, or withdrawal. Multi-topic rounds prohibited; surplus topics queue for subsequent rounds in priority order. Three binding hooks: (1) ONE decision venue per round; agent does not bundle multiple choices into a single turn. (2) Multi-ratify per round prohibited; each ratification (Concern, RC) is its own round. (3) Narrow exception: a slot 3 Concern-coverage round may produce one NC + one RC bound to the same Concern as a single decision venue (one topic, two element instantiations); this is the only multi-element round shape permitted. No other expansion. Designer-issued; intent-capture discipline depends on each round having exactly one resolvable focus.

*Type:* RULE
*Source:* designer
*Assigned ID:* RULE-17 (auto-assigned; RULE-5 slot withdrawn and unreusable)
*Relationship to NCON-2:* NCON-2 carries the must-be-true condition; this Rule carries the designer-issued constraint with same content + three hooks closing loose surfaces. Both stand.

**Loose surfaces closed by hooks**

- Hook 1 closes "agent bundles multiple choices into one turn" drift
- Hook 2 closes "multi-ratify in one round" drift (relevant to upcoming 12-RC + 12-Concern ratification phase)
- Hook 3 closes "exception expansion" drift (NC+RC pair narrowly listed, no other multi-element shapes)

- RC schema: statement + problem_anchor (Concern ID). RC carries resolve-side claim; ratification later closes the gate "Concern not covered by ratified RC or Rule."
- Per RULE-17 hook 3: slot 3 round may produce NC+RC for same Concern. NCs already exist; this round = RC-only authoring. One decision venue per round still holds.
- Cluster-D foundation defines five RC attributes: observable, designer-ratified, problem-statement-anchored, forward-looking, non-restrictive. Statement must satisfy all five.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Slot 3 round 1 — CERN-1 RC authoring**

**Concern under coverage**

- **CERN-1** (label C-2) — *Designer consumption of proof state.* Description: "Designer can read, understand, and update proof state, culminating with ratification."

**Existing NC coverage touching this Concern**

- NCON-3 — Two-yes closure gate (consumption culminates here)
- NCON-4 — Consent gate (every update routes through designer consent)

- NCON-16 — Ratification turn shape (full info packet + explicit choice)
- RULE-9, RULE-16, RULE-17 — designer-authority constraints disciplining mutations

**Proposed RC**

- *Statement:* The designer reaches two-yes closure on a closing argument by reading every active element in full text, understanding its grounding chain and collapse test, mutating the proof through designer-issued constraints and consent-token-authorized ratifications, and signing off on the closing argument that envelopes the proof state.
- *Problem anchor:* CERN-1
- *Five-attribute check:* observable (closure event = signed off), designer-ratified (designer issues ratify), problem-statement-anchored (consumption-of-proof-state is the named Concern), forward-looking (closure is downstream state), non-restrictive (doesn't constrain HOW design produces consumption capability, certifies that it works).

**Closure gate this advances**

- Removes "Concern CERN-1 not covered by ratified RC or Rule" once RC ratified. 1 of 12 Concern-coverage gates.

content.text:
RCON-1 ratified. CERN-1 closure_reason cleared automatically — RC ratification covered Concern. 11 Concerns left.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Surprise: ratifying RCON-1 cleared CERN-1's "not covered" closure_reason without separate Concern ratify call. Proof MCP treats RC ratification as the coverage event; Concern lifecycle (ratify per manage_concerns op:ratify) is a separate axis.
- Implication for designer's "going forward bundle" request: there may be no actual bundle needed. RC ratify alone covers the Concern. Concern ratify (manage_concerns op:ratify) is a separate optional lifecycle marker, possibly required by NCON-3 closure gate but not by Concern coverage gate.
- Need to verify whether closure also requires Concern lifecycle ratify or only RC ratify-via-coverage.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**State change**

- closure_reasons before ratify: 13 entries (12 Concern coverage + 1 closing-argument).
- closure_reasons after ratify: 12 entries (11 Concern coverage + 1 closing-argument).
- CERN-1 dropped from list. RC ratify alone closed the gate.

**Designer's "going forward bundle" request — re-evaluation**

Designer wanted: ratify RC + ratify Concern same round (1:1). Empirical result: ratifying RC alone closes the Concern coverage gate. No separate Concern ratify needed for closure-driver purposes.

Two interpretations:
- **(a)** "Bundle" was solving a problem that doesn't exist. RC ratify is the coverage event; no separate Concern ratify required for closure. Path: skip Concern ratify for remaining 11; just ratify RCs.
- **(b)** Concern ratify (NC-18 / RULE-6 lifecycle) still has paper-trail value separate from coverage. Designer may still want it for completeness.

**Closure gate check**

Active closure_reasons remaining:
- 11 Concerns × "not covered by ratified RC or Rule"
- 1 × closing-argument not presented

No "Concerns must be ratified" reason. Concern ratify (manage_concerns op:ratify) appears to be optional for closure under D.2 schema. Cluster-D-1 NC-18 may or may not gate closure; empirical answer: not gating here.

**Path options**

- **(a)** RC-ratify-only mode: per Concern, author RC + ratify RC. 2 rounds × 11 Concerns = 22 rounds. Concern ratify skipped.
- **(b)** Originally proposed bundle (RC ratify + Concern ratify same round): need Permission to RULE-17 hook 2.
- **(c)** Sequential: author RC, ratify RC, ratify Concern. 3 rounds × 11 Concerns = 33 rounds.

(a) is fastest if Concern ratify carries no load-bearing weight; verify intent before locking path.

Going-forward bundle request still open. Path?

→ Solve Step 16 — RCON-1 ratified, CERN-1 covered

What is the current rule for RC approval and what is a recommended change to either allow separate approval or grouped approval?

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Reflecting on flow: designer's stated intent was Concerns ratified up-front (drives planning) → RCs ratified at end. Actual session flow used draft Concerns through planning — it worked, planning didn't need ratified Concerns.
- Empirical: ratifying RC alone closes the coverage gate. Concern ratify (manage_concerns op:ratify) is separate axis — not gating D.2 closure currently.
- Mismatch: original intent called for two ratify cadences (early + late). Session reality used neither up-front; both pending now at end. Designer's "going forward bundle" request reframes intent into late-stage 1:1 grouping.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Current rule for RC approval (state of proof)**

No single Rule explicitly governs RC approval semantics. Effective rule emerges from intersection:

- **RULE-16 hook 3** — ONE operation per state-mutating tool call. ratify_resolve_condition takes one element_id; no batch.
- **RULE-17 hook 2** — Multi-ratify per round prohibited. Each ratify (Concern, RC) is its own round.
- **NCON-3** (two-yes closure gate) — closure requires designer twice (present + go).
- **NCON-4 / RULE-16** — consent token required, source: designer.
- **Implicit closure gate (proof MCP)** — Concern is "covered" when RC anchored to it is ratified OR a Rule covers it. Concern coverage = closure gate; Concern lifecycle ratify (separate manage_concerns op:ratify) is not a current closure gate in D.2.

So the rule that fired for RCON-1 was: ratify_resolve_condition for one RC = one round; this single ratify alone closed both the unratified-RC warning AND the Concern-coverage gate for the anchored Concern. Concern lifecycle remained in `draft`.

**Recommended change**

Two paths cover designer's two stated intents (separate vs. grouped). Both viable; one preferred.

**Option A — Separate approval cadence rule**

- Add Rule: "Concern lifecycle ratify (manage_concerns op:ratify) occurs at Concern input/first-review (Phase 2 or early Phase 4), independent of RC ratify. RC ratify (ratify_resolve_condition) occurs at completion (late Phase 4). Both bound by RULE-17 hook 2 — each its own round."
- For this session: bulk-ratify 12 Concerns now in 12 separate rounds (catch-up), then proceed with 11 remaining RC author/ratify rounds.
- Round budget remaining: 12 + (2 × 11) = 34 rounds.
- Honors original stated intent. Heavy late-stage cost because session entered late stage with no Concerns ratified.

**Option B — Grouped 1:1 bundling Permission (recommended)**

- Add Permission relieving RULE-17 hook 2: "Bundle ratify_resolve_condition + manage_concerns op:ratify in same round when the RC's problem_anchor = the Concern's id (strict 1:1). Only this pair shape; no other multi-ratify combinations. Applies during completion-phase ratification."
- For this session: each remaining Concern gets one round = author RC + ratify RC + ratify Concern; or two rounds = author RC, then bundled ratify+ratify.
- Round budget remaining: 11 author rounds + 11 bundled ratify rounds = 22 rounds (saves 12 vs. Option A).
- Doesn't change RULE-17 hook 2 globally; narrow Permission carries the exception. Permission element type (cluster-D-1 schema) explicitly designed for this kind of relief.
- Matches designer's "going forward bundle" request from prior turn.
- Also need to handle CERN-1 retroactively: ratify CERN-1 in its own round (cannot bundle since RCON-1 already ratified). One catch-up round.

**Why Option B preferred**

- Permission narrowly scoped — relief is 1:1 RC+Concern only; no slippery slope to general multi-ratify.
- Round budget 22 vs. 34. Real savings on a session already long.
- Honors designer's retrospective reframing in last turn ("going forward, ratify RC and approve concern same round").
- Keeps RULE-17 hook 2 intact for all other ratify shapes.
- Explicit Permission element creates paper trail of the exception and the reason.

**Choice (per NCON-16)**

- (a) accept Option B (recommended) — add Permission element relieving RULE-17 hook 2 narrowly; 1:1 bundling for completion-phase RC+Concern ratification
- (b) accept Option A — add Rule for separate approval cadence; bulk-ratify Concerns now then proceed
- (c) push back with revisions to either option's text
- (d) defer

Awaiting explicit accept naming target.

→ Solve Step 16 — RC approval rule analysis

content.text:
PERM-1 accepted. Round 16. permission_count=1, ratified_rc_count=1.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- First Permission element in proof. PERM-1 narrowly relieves RULE-17 hook 2 for the 1:1 bundle case. Permission carries paper trail of the exception with explicit scope clause.
- Stall detector still firing on NC count flat — low-signal alarm, work has shifted to RC/Permission/Ratify class. Will keep ignoring per prior turn.
- Path 1 (early Concern ratify pattern) still pending. Next round = path 1 staging.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Slot 7 round — path 1 proposal**

Path 1 = "early in design, present list of concerns, ask designer to accept = approved." This is a turn-shape discipline for Concern-creation phase, not a ratify mechanism. Proposes: when Concerns are seeded (Phase 2 open_proof) or surfaced mid-cycle (slot 7 agent-proposed), agent presents the Concern + asks designer to ratify in the same round, locking it on creation.

**Element type choice**

- **NC (necessary condition)** — frames as must-be-true claim about the Phase 2 turn shape. Pairs with NCON-12 (Agent prepares open_proof seed) and NCON-15 (NC authoring discipline). NC-class fits because this is a structural condition of how the Phase 2 boundary works.
- **Rule** — frames as designer-issued constraint authority. Designer-issued. Could pair with RULE-9 (only designer creates Rules).

NC fits better — Phase 2 turn shape is a structural condition, not a constraint on designer authority. Authority already covered by RULE-9 + RULE-16.

**Draft NC text**

*Statement:* When Concerns are seeded at Phase 2 (open_proof) or surfaced mid-cycle via slot 7 agent-proposed topics, the agent presents each Concern in full text (label + description) and explicitly requests designer ratify per NCON-16 turn shape. Designer accept on a Concern at presentation = ratified Concern (manage_concerns op:ratify fires same round under RULE-16 strict consent). Concerns presented but not explicitly accepted in the same round remain in draft. Concerns left in draft at RC review fall under PERM-1 bundling — RC ratify + Concern ratify in same round permitted for 1:1 case. Concerns ratified up-front under this NC do not bundle at RC review; their RC ratify stands alone.

*Grounding:* RULE-2 (Shared Understanding), RULE-3 (PM-as-decider), RULE-16 (consent gate), NCON-16 (ratification turn shape).

*Reasoning chain:* IF Shared Understanding requires designer-locked Concerns to ground proof work (RULE-2) AND PM-as-decider says designer authoritatively names the targets the proof addresses (RULE-3) AND ratification turn shape requires explicit accept on lockable elements (NCON-16) AND consent must be explicit per RULE-16 hooks, THEN Concerns presented at Phase 2 or via slot 7 must (a) be presented in full text per NCON-16, (b) include explicit ratify request, (c) ratify on accept in the same round, (d) remain in draft if not accepted. PERM-1 covers the late-bundle path for Concerns that reach RC review still in draft.

*Collapse test:* Without this NC, designer faces two failure modes — (1) Concerns added at Phase 2 stay in draft indefinitely with no agent prompt for ratification, requiring designer to volunteer ratify out of context (PM-as-decider inverts: agent should prompt the locking moment); (2) RC ratify becomes the de facto Concern lock per the empirical CERN-1 pattern, collapsing Concern lifecycle into RC lifecycle and erasing the up-front-locks-direction discipline designer originally intended.

*Rejected alternatives:*
- Concerns auto-ratify on add — inverts PM-as-decider; agent silently locks designer's authority moment.
- Concerns require ratify-on-add for the proof to accept the add — too rigid; sometimes designer adds a Concern intentionally as draft for later refinement.
- Concerns never ratify up-front; only bundle at RC review — collapses the early-direction-setting designer wanted; Phase 2 Concerns drive Phase 3+ research and round selection, value of locking them up-front is real.
- This discipline as a Rule rather than NC — Rule-class is for designer-issued constraints; this is a structural condition on agent turn shape, NC-class fits.

**Choice (per NCON-16)**

- (a) accept and fire add NC op
- (b) push back with revisions to NC text
- (c) defer
- (d) author as Rule instead of NC

Awaiting explicit accept naming target.

→ Solve Step 17 — path 1 NC staging
